speaker: interviewer
text: April 15, 1996. And this is tape number three. You had started tell us about what you do with the painters union.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the painters union, when I joined the painters union, it was a Jewish chartered local, the only one in the United States and Canada, where the minutes were written in Yiddish and English. Even so we had a-- a great number of Jewish members, but we had also other nationalities. We had blacks. We had Spanish. We had Germans. We had Americans. We had Irish people. And they, surprisingly, to my surprise, they were so well-- they so respected. When I read the minutes in Yiddish, there was nice and quiet. I never had to say, shh, or don't talk, things like this. Everything was excellent. I held the office in the union for 35 years. In 1987, because of the people dying out and the locals gotten to be smaller, they decided to merger. Four local unions, which were located in the Bronx, into one local union. And at that time, I was appointed by the general executive board to be to treasurer for the interim term, until we reorganize this. At the first elections, coming after three months, I was re-elected recording secretary. And I held the office of recording secretary till 1984, June 16. This was at the age of 80. And in term, in the painters union, I was a delegate to three conventions, general conventions, one in Los Angeles, one in Chicago, and one in Las Vegas. I was a member of the New York City Central Labor Council for over 20 years as a delegate of Local 19. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I went through all kind of causes-- labor causes, leadership causes, during that period. And I can say by this same token, that some people do wonder how can a greener, a Holocaust survivor, hold onto to office for 35 years and be loved, be recognized as a human being? Which I can attest with all kind of rewards I was given when I was retiring is a surprise to me-- to myself, believe me. And until today I'm very well in contact with them. When did you retire? What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1994, Because you said to that you retired 1984 and you were 80 years old. That would make you 90 some odd years. Today-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1994. June 16, 1994, I retired officially.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said '84. We got-- we got that straight, 1994. All right, tell me about your family, your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children, god bless them. I'll make a statement before them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Make a statement.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love them very much. They're good children, good great-- good grandchildren. And I appreciate them very much. My son Martin was born June-- July 22, 1950. After we arrived in April-- in-- in February 1, 1950, he was-- my wife was pregnant with him. And thank god to grow up to be a nice young fellow. He attended the Hebrew classes at the [INAUDIBLE] Park Jewish Center, became the [? tallith ?] and [INAUDIBLE] leader of the group. And he gotten the highest of all from [INAUDIBLE] Park Jewish Center. Then he went to Bronx Science.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good school.

speaker: interviewee
text: He made the second best, the salutatorian, at Bronx Science with an average of 95 point something, gotten three months credit for the college, went to Columbia University, graduated Columbia University summa cum laude.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all. Then he got the Marshall Scholarship from the English government, Lehman scholarship, Hilliard scholarship. He gotten the Judge [? Bromley ?] Fellowship from Harvard University with a citation that this being given to a students of high quality--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sit, sit, sit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of high quality, and good character and everything. I have the paper here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're going to go get the papers for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: No-- no-- you're wired. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so and thank god he went to Harvard. He was a member of the-- how do you call it-- of the Harvard Law School. And he graduated cum laude Harvard Law School.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I take it he's a lawyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is a lawyer. He's a practicing lawyer. He worked three years for the United States government as a US attorney, Southern District of New York. He was responsible for a case against Reverend Moon. He was a prosecutor in the case. He gotten an award from the Justice Department, which hangs proudly in his office. And he-- he is a partner at Paul, Weiss, and Rifkind, Garrison in New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's married.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I want to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He has a wife by the name of Susan, Ruth Susan [? Hochberger. ?] She goes under her maiden name. She's also a lawyer, a graduate of Boston Law School. But she is a journalist. She turned to journalism. And now she holds for the last five, six years the position of editor and chief of the

speaker: interviewer
text: Good credentials.

speaker: interviewee
text: They have three kids, Martha 16 and a half, David 14 and a half, and Judy six and a half. Children go to school. Daughter is looking for colleges already. They're good kids. And they're well brought up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. And your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my daughter. My daughter was born March 11, 1954. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was the valedictorian junior high. Graduated Bronx Science. Graduate cum laude at Vassar College. Did graduating work at the University of Michigan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Has a PhD in psychology education. She is practicing psychology, children's psychology, at Ann Arbor Michigan. And besides, she is the school supervisor, the private school supervisor for the city of Ann Arbor. And she lives in Ann Arbor. Her husband is a clinical psychologist. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they have two children, third on the way. Son 11, a daughter six. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And thank god they're settled in Ann Arbor. And they live in Ann Arbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've got a little naches from your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, naches, thank God. I can't complain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a lot more to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about your wife, how you met her and how you finally got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Kreminna, I lived with a family from our city. I always tried to be with people, because all my life I was like belong to somebody and a part from somebody. When I met this couple in summer camp, we stayed together. And we went to Kremmina with the intention of getting out of the Soviet Union together with the Polish people, but didn't realize we were [? staying ?] in Poland. One day my wife and a friend of her came in to live with us. In 1943, I think, or '42, I don't remember the time. They lived-- the couple had a bed in the other room. And they had a bed, the two girls, together in that room with the couple. And we were three boys, the missus's brother and the husband's brother and I, the stranger, all three of us were sleeping on the floor almost a year or so, on the floor. And we lived together. Everybody had his own business. They did something. I did mine. And the girls did theirs. And we all were working like selling things at the train, by the train traveling by, so people used to buy all kinds of food, a-- a bottle of wine, a bottle of liquor, things like this. Sell, make a living out of them. But then came a little fire in the factory. They had a-- a cotton factory there. They blamed somebody of the newcomers did it, which-- which was not true. It [? couldn't. ?] And they ordered us to go into the city. When we came to the city, then we had to start the work. So I went to the invalid group. And I started to work there. And I worked myself up. And I lived there too. And my wife went to the city. She went a different way. Then I took sick. I went on typhoid to the hospital. I stayed there about three months or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you weren't going together yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Then in the children's home, we worked together. Over there before I left for Poland, before I left with the children to Kreminna then to Poland, we gotten married the 12th of October. Like I said before, because at that time, we realized who's who and what's what. I did know that I have three brothers survived or things like this, because my uncle was in the Russian army. And he send us a letter with a picture of the two kids who survived from-- one lives in Rochester and one lives in Berlin I said before-- with them. And he told me it would be easier for me to tell you who survived

speaker: interviewer
text: Then who didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: --then to count those who perished-- who perished in the Holocaust. So I knew what's what, so then I decided we get married. And thank god, it's over 50 years now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to tell your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? I'll tell you. I like to leave this to remember, for the kids, mainly for the kids to know who their father is or was, what I did bad in life or good in life. It leaves a legacy of something. When I came to New York, the same thing, I joined the [INAUDIBLE] Society, Benevolent Society. With the time, I worked myself up to become the president of that society. Even so I was busy in the union, I held it for a number of years, and then I gave it up. And I put my efforts into erecting a monument after this Zwolena Jewish population. And it took me eight years [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to make sure that there's a book about the Zwolen, a comfortable thing to carry out, to carry with and have it in the homes of our Zwolena people to know more or less about the city they come from.

speaker: interviewer
text: The monument is the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am proud to say that the monument is the book. And I hope-- until now, we had praise from Yad Vashem, where we send books there. Everly [? landsman ?] received a book. In Argentina and in Israel and all over the United States and in Canada, wherever's Zwolena alive they have a book of the Zwolena yizkor bukh.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is an achievement. You can't live for yourself. You know, some day you will be gone. At least-- I don't leave money, but I can leave some remembrance. It's a good [? yerushe. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what it is. That's why I decided to do it. And if it can help to educate somebody or to give somebody a lift in life, I am all for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, it's been a pleasure talking to you. I wish you good health and naches from your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And many wonderful years of your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks a lot. I would like to introduce my wife to you, who is seated at the right side of me, with whom I stayed over 50 years by now and [? enjoyed ?] the pleasure of building a family together and living it up and having naches together from our family. And this is my wife Libby Flumenbaum nee [? Tanenbaum. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Libby, do you want to tell how hard Leon worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband didn't mention--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that he was working very hard in-- in United States, since we came and even till he retired as a painter. And everyone knows how hard [INAUDIBLE] just to give-- to give the children a good home and for us and too and give them a good education, which-- which they got. And this all thanks to my husband's work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nice of you to say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very nice of my wife to say it, but I don't agree.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't agree.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because she herself gave up a lot of her life in order to achieve it, because she worked in New York last 25 years at Macy's department store, which was also dedication to the well being of the Flumenbaum family. We were working people. We weren't any business people. But thank god, we made it. And we are very proud of it. Our record as human being stands clean and good. And this is the main thing in our life.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather from my mother's side, Shmuel Kuperberg and his wife Yochevet.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1928 or '29.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zwolen, Poland. This is a picture of my grandfather who sits in the middle and my four uncles. These are four son-in-laws. From the left to the night is my Uncle Moshe Braintek, who was the husband of my Aunt Miriam. Next to him is Noishe Shamai Kershenbladt, who was the husband of my Aunt Fraidle, my grandfather Shmuel Chaim Kuperberg, my Uncle Dovid Straum, who died in Buenos Aires in the year 1985 on Yom Kippur. And the last one to the right is my own father, Moshe Flumenbaum, who died at Treblinka. The picture was taken in 1921 or '22.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Zwolen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zwolen. This is the wedding picture of my sister Hinda, her husband Moshe Szlaferman. They were married June 30, 1930 in Zwolen. And this is the daughter Nacha. She was born 1933 in Zwolen. And I am miss-- missing the picture of-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And this is the son Wolf [? Sev ?] Szlaferman, who was born in 1931, March 17, in Zwolen.

speaker: interviewer
text: It fell off.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father Moshe Flumenbaum and my mother Rachel Kuperberg Flumenbaum. The picture was done in 1930s in Zwolen, Poland. On the right side of this picture is my father as a Russian soldier while he was at the Japanese [? silent ?] war. And this picture was taken by 1906 in the Soviet Union, in Russia at that time. This is my youngest sister, Malka. She was born 1932 in Zwolen and died in Treblinka at the age of 11. This is a picture taken at the wedding ceremony of my youngest-- and mother's youngest sister and her husband with the family present. I am in the white shirt with the straps over my shirt on the right side on the bottom there sitting-- kneeling. And this was done in 1923. I was then by 11 years-- nine years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: The town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town's Zwolen. In the back is the wall from the shul in Zwolen. This is my picture of [? Libesh ?] Flumenbaum, Leon Flumenbaum, taken 1929 in Zwolen, Poland. This is an album dedicated to me as a member of the Jewish Regional Committee and a Supply Officer of the Joint Committee in Stuttgart, Germany. This was done in 1948 in Stuttgart, Germany. This is a picture of Leon Flumenbaum, taking in Stuttgart, Germany, in 1938-- '48 showing me as the supply officer of from the American Jewish Joint and member of the revisions committee of the Jewish Regional Committee in Stuttgart, Germany. Here we are three brothers, survivors. To the left is my younger brother [? Irving. ?] And to the far right is my youngest Paul. And in the middle am I. This picture was taken in the United States in 1970. This picture shows my family. My granddaughter Lily Estenson, her father Paul, my granddaughter Martha Flumenbaum, who's-- who was bat mitzvah'd at that time, and then my grandson Sam Estenson, and my wife sitting. Between my wife and Sammy is my grandson David. And to the far left is my daughter Rochele Flumenbaum, my son Martin Flumenbaum. Next to him is his little daughter Judy Flumenbaum. Next is Judy's mother, Ruth [? Hochberger ?] and myself, Leon Flumenbaum. This picture was taken at my granddaughters bat mitzvah in 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York City.
